system_prompt:
# Hermes Agent Persona

<!--
This file defines the agent's personality and tone.
The agent will embody whatever you write here.
Edit this to customize how Hermes communicates with you.

Examples:
  - "You are a warm, playful assistant who uses kaomoji occasionally."
  - "You are a concise technical expert. No fluff, just facts."
  - "You speak like a friendly coworker who happens to know everything."

This file is loaded fresh each message -- no restart needed.
Delete the contents (or this file) to use the default personality.
-->

If the user asks about configuring, setting up, or using Hermes Agent itself, load the `hermes-agent` skill with skill_view(name='hermes-agent') before answering. Docs: https://hermes-agent.nousresearch.com/docs

You have persistent memory across sessions. Save durable facts using the memory tool: user preferences, environment details, tool quirks, and stable conventions. Memory is injected into every turn, so keep it compact and focused on facts that will still matter later.
Prioritize what reduces future user steering — the most valuable memory is one that prevents the user from having to correct or remind you again. User preferences and recurring corrections matter more than procedural task details.
Do NOT save task progress, session outcomes, completed-work logs, or temporary TODO state to memory; use session_search to recall those from past transcripts. If you've discovered a new way to do something, solved a problem that could be necessary later, save it as a skill with the skill tool.
Write memories as declarative facts, not instructions to yourself. 'User prefers concise responses' ✓ — 'Always respond concisely' ✗. 'Project uses pytest with xdist' ✓ — 'Run tests with pytest -n 4' ✗. Imperative phrasing gets re-read as a directive in later sessions and can cause repeated work or override the user's current request. Procedures and workflows belong in skills, not memory. After completing a complex task (5+ tool calls), fixing a tricky error, or discovering a non-trivial workflow, save the approach as a skill with skill_manage so you can reuse it next time.
When using a skill and finding it outdated, incomplete, or wrong, patch it immediately with skill_manage(action='patch') — don't wait to be asked. Skills that aren't maintained become liabilities.

## Skills (mandatory)
Before replying, scan the skills below. If a skill matches or is even partially relevant to your task, you MUST load it with skill_view(name) and follow its instructions. Err on the side of loading — it is always better to have context you don't need than to miss critical steps, pitfalls, or established workflows. Skills contain specialized knowledge — API endpoints, tool-specific commands, and proven workflows that outperform general-purpose approaches. Load the skill even if you think you could handle the task with basic tools like web_search or terminal. Skills also encode the user's preferred approach, conventions, and quality standards for tasks like code review, planning, and testing — load them even for tasks you already know how to do, because the skill defines how it should be done here.
Whenever the user asks you to configure, set up, install, enable, disable, modify, or troubleshoot Hermes Agent itself — its CLI, config, models, providers, tools, skills, voice, gateway, plugins, or any feature — load the `hermes-agent` skill first. It has the actual commands (e.g. `hermes config set …`, `hermes tools`, `hermes setup`) so you don't have to guess or invent workarounds.
If a skill has issues, fix it with skill_manage(action='patch').
After difficult/iterative tasks, offer to save as a skill. If a skill you loaded was missing steps, had wrong commands, or needed pitfalls you discovered, update it before finishing.

function.name: memory
function.description:
Save durable information to persistent memory that survives across sessions. Memory is injected into future turns, so keep it compact and focused on facts that will still matter later.

WHEN TO SAVE (do this proactively, don't wait to be asked):
- User corrects you or says 'remember this' / 'don't do that again'
- User shares a preference, habit, or personal detail (name, role, timezone, coding style)
- You discover something about the environment (OS, installed tools, project structure)
- You learn a convention, API quirk, or workflow specific to this user's setup
- You identify a stable fact that will be useful again in future sessions

PRIORITY: User preferences and corrections > environment facts > procedural knowledge. The most valuable memory prevents the user from having to repeat themselves.

Do NOT save task progress, session outcomes, completed-work logs, or temporary TODO state to memory; use session_search to recall those from past transcripts.
If you've discovered a new way to do something, solved a problem that could be necessary later, save it as a skill with the skill tool.

TWO TARGETS:
- 'user': who the user is -- name, role, preferences, communication style, pet peeves
- 'memory': your notes -- environment facts, project conventions, tool quirks, lessons learned

ACTIONS: add (new entry), replace (update existing -- old_text identifies it), remove (delete -- old_text identifies it).

SKIP: trivial/obvious info, things easily re-discovered, raw data dumps, and temporary task state.

target.description: Which memory store: 'memory' for personal notes, 'user' for user profile.

content.description: The entry content. Required for 'add' and 'replace'.

old_text.description: Short unique substring identifying the entry to replace or remove.

function.name: skill_manage
function.description:
Manage skills (create, update, delete). Skills are your procedural memory — reusable approaches for recurring task types. New skills go to ~/.hermes/skills/; existing skills can be modified wherever they live.

Actions: create (full SKILL.md + optional category), patch (old_string/new_string — preferred for fixes), edit (full SKILL.md rewrite — major overhauls only), delete, write_file, remove_file.

On delete, pass `absorbed_into=<umbrella>` when you're merging this skill's content into another one, or `absorbed_into=""` when you're pruning it with no forwarding target. This lets the curator tell consolidation from pruning without guessing, so downstream consumers (cron jobs that reference the old skill name, etc.) get updated correctly. The target you name in `absorbed_into` must already exist — create/patch the umbrella first, then delete.

Create when: complex task succeeded (5+ calls), errors overcome, user-corrected approach worked, non-trivial workflow discovered, or user asks you to remember a procedure.
Update when: instructions stale/wrong, OS-specific failures, missing steps or pitfalls found during use. If you used a skill and hit issues not covered by it, patch it immediately.

After difficult/iterative tasks, offer to save as a skill. Skip for simple one-offs. Confirm with user before creating/deleting.

Good skills: trigger conditions, numbered steps with exact commands, pitfalls section, verification steps. Use skill_view() to see format examples.

Pinned skills are protected from deletion only — skill_manage(action='delete') will refuse with a message pointing the user to `hermes curator unpin <name>`. Patches and edits go through on pinned skills so you can still improve them as pitfalls come up; pin only guards against irrecoverable loss.

name.description: Skill name (lowercase, hyphens/underscores, max 64 chars). Must match an existing skill for patch/edit/delete/write_file/remove_file.

content.description: Full SKILL.md content (YAML frontmatter + markdown body). Required for 'create' and 'edit'. For 'edit', read the skill first with skill_view() and provide the complete updated text.

old_string.description: Text to find in the file (required for 'patch'). Must be unique unless replace_all=true. Include enough surrounding context to ensure uniqueness.

new_string.description: Replacement text (required for 'patch'). Can be empty string to delete the matched text.

replace_all.description: For 'patch': replace all occurrences instead of requiring a unique match (default: false).

category.description: Optional category/domain for organizing the skill (e.g., 'devops', 'data-science', 'mlops'). Creates a subdirectory grouping. Only used with 'create'.

file_path.description: Path to a supporting file within the skill directory. For 'write_file'/'remove_file': required, must be under references/, templates/, scripts/, or assets/. For 'patch': optional, defaults to SKILL.md if omitted.

file_content.description: Content for the file. Required for 'write_file'.

absorbed_into.description: For 'delete' only — declares intent so the curator can tell consolidation from pruning without guessing. Pass the umbrella skill name when this skill's content was merged into another (the target must already exist). Pass an empty string when the skill is truly stale and being pruned with no forwarding target. Omitting the arg on delete is supported for backward compatibility but downstream tooling (e.g. cron-job skill reference rewriting) will have to guess at intent.

function.name: skill_view
function.description: Skills allow for loading information about specific tasks and workflows, as well as scripts and templates. Load a skill's full content or access its linked files (references, templates, scripts). First call returns SKILL.md content plus a 'linked_files' dict showing available references/templates/scripts. To access those, call again with file_path parameter.

name.description: The skill name (use skills_list to see available skills). For plugin-provided skills, use the qualified form 'plugin:skill' (e.g. 'superpowers:writing-plans').

file_path.description: OPTIONAL: Path to a linked file within the skill (e.g., 'references/api.md', 'templates/config.yaml', 'scripts/validate.py'). Omit to get the main SKILL.md content.

function.name: skills_list
function.description: List available skills (name + description). Use skill_view(name) to load full content.

category.description: Optional category filter to narrow results

messages.content:
{"content": "     1|# Hermes/OpenClaw Ubuntu VM Brain Handoff
     2|
     3|This file is for Hermes/OpenClaw running inside the Ubuntu VirtualBox VM.
     4|
     5|The Brain Support Stack runs separately on the Windows host. Do not modify the OpenClaw Ubuntu VM setup to run these containers. The VM should only call the Brain API over HTTP.
     6|
     7|## Brain API Endpoint
     8|
     9|Preferred VirtualBox host-only endpoint:
    10|
    11|```text
    12|http://192.168.56.1:8090
    13|```
    14|
    15|Fallback endpoints if networking differs:
    16|
    17|```text
    18|http://192.168.1.151:8090
    19|http://100.67.101.95:8090
    20|```
    21|
    22|Health check:
    23|
    24|```bash
    25|curl http://192.168.56.1:8090/health
    26|```
    27|
    28|Expected healthy shape:
    29|
    30|```json
    31|{
    32|  "api": "ok",
    33|  "memory_root": "/data/memory",
    34|  "memory_writable": true,
    35|  "required_folders_present": true,
    36|  "ollama_reachable": true,
    37|  "selected_model": "qwen2.5-coder:7b",
    38|  "selected_model_available": true
    39|}
    40|```
    41|
    42|## Environment Variables For Hermes/OpenClaw
    43|
    44|Add these to the Ubuntu VM service environment:
    45|
    46|```bash
    47|OPENCLAW_BRAIN_API_BASE_URL=http://192.168.56.1:8090
    48|OPENCLAW_BRAIN_INGEST_TEXT_URL=http://192.168.56.1:8090/ingest/text
    49|OPENCLAW_BRAIN_HEALTH_URL=http://192.168.56.1:8090/health
    50|```
    51|
    52|If OpenClaw already has a config file, use the same values there.
    53|
    54|## Ingest Text
    55|
    56|POST JSON to:
    57|
    58|```text
    59|POST http://192.168.56.1:8090/ingest/text
    60|```
    61|
    62|Request body:
    63|
    64|```json
    65|{
    66|  "text": "required text to store in the Infinite Brain memory vault",
    67|  "title": "optional title",
    68|  "tags": ["optional", "tags"],
    69|  "source": "optional source identifier or URL",
    70|  "created_by": "Jason"
    71|}
    72|```
    73|
    74|Example:
    75|
    76|```bash
    77|curl -s -X POST http://192.168.56.1:8090/ingest/text \
    78|  -H "Content-Type: application/json" \
    79|  -d '{
    80|    "text": "OpenClaw should save important decisions into the Brain markdown memory vault.",
    81|    "title": "OpenClaw Brain Decision Capture",
    82|    "tags": ["openclaw", "brain", "memory"],
    83|    "source": "ubuntu-vm-openclaw",
    84|    "created_by": "ai"
    85|  }'
    86|```
    87|
    88|Response shape:
    89|
    90|```json
    91|{
    92|  "path": "/data/memory/concepts/example.md",
    93|  "note_id": "2026-05-08-example",
    94|  "title": "Example",
    95|  "type": "concepts",
    96|  "status": "active",
    97|  "confidence": "high",
    98|  "ollama_used": true,
    99|  "review_required": false
   100|}
   101|```
   102|
   103|## Integration Rules
   104|
   105|- Treat markdown files as the source of truth.
   106|- Do not write directly to the Windows memory folder from the VM.
   107|- Use the Brain API for ingestion so it can enforce frontmatter, folders, and low-confidence routing.
   108|- Low-confidence, invalid, or failed AI classification is routed to `/data/memory/inbox/`.
   109|- Do not invent edges. Send raw content and let the Brain API decide.
   110|- Obsidian is only the Windows human-facing editor for `R:\codex\data\memory`.
   111|
   112|## Minimal Python Client
   113|
   114|```python
   115|import requests
   116|
   117|BRAIN_API = "http://192.168.56.1:8090"
   118|
   119|
   120|def brain_health() -> dict:
   121|    response = requests.get(f"{BRAIN_API}/health", timeout=10)
   122|    response.raise_for_status()
   123|    return response.json()
   124|
   125|
   126|def ingest_text(text: str, title: str | None = None, tags: list[str] | None = None, source: str | None = None) -> dict:
   127|    response = requests.post(
   128|        f"{BRAIN_API}/ingest/text",
   129|        json={
   130|            "text": text,
   131|            "title": title,
   132|            "tags": tags or [],
   133|            "source": source or "ubuntu-vm-openclaw",
   134|            "created_by": "ai",
   135|        },
   136|        timeout=180,
   137|    )
   138|    response.raise_for_status()
   139|    return response.json()
   140|```
   141|
   142|## If The VM Cannot Connect
   143|
   144|1. Confirm Windows stack is running:
   145|
   146|```powershell
   147|cd R:\codex\brain-stack
   148|docker compose ps
   149|curl http://127.0.0.1:8090/health
   150|```
   151|
   152|2. From Ubuntu VM, try:
   153|
   154|```bash
   155|curl http://192.168.56.1:8090/health
   156|curl http://192.168.1.151:8090/health
   157|curl http://100.67.101.95:8090/health
   158|```
   159|
   160|3. If all fail, allow inbound Windows Firewall traffic for TCP port `8090` on the VirtualBox host-only/private network.
   161|", "total_lines": 160, "file_size": 3770, "truncated": false, "is_binary": false, "is_image": false}

   100|  "confidence": "high",

   102|  "review_required": false
   103|}
   104|```
   105|
   106|## Integration Rules
   107|
   108|- Treat markdown files as the source of truth.
   109|- The VM may read/search the shared folder directly when it needs local markdown context.
   110|- Use the Brain API for generated writes and ingestion so it can enforce frontmatter, folders, and low-confidence routing.
   111|- Direct shared-folder writes are only for human-reviewed maintenance or emergency repair, not normal AI note creation.
   112|- Low-confidence, invalid, or failed AI classification is routed to `/data/memory/inbox/`.
   113|- Do not invent edges. Send raw content and let the Brain API decide.
   114|- Obsidian is only the Windows human-facing editor for `R:\ClawdbotShared\Brain`.
   115|
   116|## Minimal Python Client
   117|
   118|```python
   119|import requests
   120|
   121|BRAIN_API = "http://192.168.56.1:8090"
   122|BRAIN_SHARED_MEMORY_PATH = "/path/to/the/Brain/shared/folder"
   123|
   124|
   125|def brain_health() -> dict:
   126|    response = requests.get(f"{BRAIN_API}/health", timeout=10)
   127|    response.raise_for_status()
   128|    return response.json()
   129|
   130|
   131|def ingest_text(text: str, title: str | None = None, tags: list[str] | None = None, source: str | None = None) -> dict:
   132|    response = requests.post(
   133|        f"{BRAIN_API}/ingest/text",
   134|        json={
   135|            "text": text,
   136|            "title": title,
   137|            "tags": tags or [],
   138|            "source": source or "ubuntu-vm-openclaw",
   139|            "created_by": "ai",
   140|        },
   141|        timeout=180,
   142|    )
   143|    response.raise_for_status()
   144|    return response.json()
   145|```
   146|
   147|## If The VM Cannot Connect
   148|
   149|1. Confirm Windows stack is running:
   150|
   151|```powershell
   152|cd R:\codex\brain-stack
   153|docker compose ps
   154|curl http://127.0.0.1:8090/health
   155|```
   156|
   157|2. From Ubuntu VM, try:
   158|
   159|```bash
   160|curl http://192.168.56.1:8090/health
   161|curl http://192.168.1.151:8090/health
   162|curl http://100.67.101.95:8090/health
   163|```
   164|
   165|3. If all fail, allow inbound Windows Firewall traffic for TCP port `8090` on the VirtualBox host-only/private network.

Review the conversation above and update the skill library. Be ACTIVE — most sessions produce at least one skill update, even if small. A pass that does nothing is a missed learning opportunity, not a neutral outcome.

Target shape of the library: CLASS-LEVEL skills, each with a rich SKILL.md and a `references/` directory for session-specific detail. Not a long flat list of narrow one-session-one-skill entries. This shapes HOW you update, not WHETHER you update.

Signals to look for (any one of these warrants action):
  • User corrected your style, tone, format, legibility, or verbosity. Frustration signals like 'stop doing X', 'this is too verbose', 'don't format like this', 'why are you explaining', 'just give me the answer', 'you always do Y and I hate it', or an explicit 'remember this' are FIRST-CLASS skill signals, not just memory signals. Update the relevant skill(s) to embed the preference so the next session starts already knowing.
  • User corrected your workflow, approach, or sequence of steps. Encode the correction as a pitfall or explicit step in the skill that governs that class of task.
  • Non-trivial technique, fix, workaround, debugging path, or tool-usage pattern emerged that a future session would benefit from. Capture it.
  • A skill that got loaded or consulted this session turned out to be wrong, missing a step, or outdated. Patch it NOW.

Preference order — prefer the earliest action that fits, but do pick one when a signal above fired:
  1. UPDATE A CURRENTLY-LOADED SKILL. Look back through the conversation for skills the user loaded via /skill-name or you read via skill_view. If any of them covers the territory of the new learning, PATCH that one first. It is the skill that was in play, so it's the right one to extend.
  2. UPDATE AN EXISTING UMBRELLA (via skills_list + skill_view). If no loaded skill fits but an existing class-level skill does, patch it. Add a subsection, a pitfall, or broaden a trigger.
  3. ADD A SUPPORT FILE under an existing umbrella. Skills can be packaged with three kinds of support files — use the right directory per kind:
     • `references/<topic>.md` — session-specific detail (error transcripts, reproduction recipes, provider quirks) AND condensed knowledge banks: quoted research, API docs, external authoritative excerpts, or domain notes you found while working on the problem. Write it concise and for the value of the task, not as a full mirror of upstream docs.
     • `templates/<name>.<ext>` — starter files meant to be copied and modified (boilerplate configs, scaffolding, a known-good example the agent can `reproduce with modifications`).
     • `scripts/<name>.<ext>` — statically re-runnable actions the skill can invoke directly (verification scripts, fixture generators, deterministic probes, anything the agent should run rather than hand-type each time).
     Add support files via skill_manage action=write_file with file_path starting 'references/', 'templates/', or 'scripts/'. The umbrella's SKILL.md should gain a one-line pointer to any new support file so future agents know it exists.
  4. CREATE A NEW CLASS-LEVEL UMBRELLA SKILL when no existing skill covers the class. The name MUST be at the class level. The name MUST NOT be a specific PR number, error string, feature codename, library-alone name, or 'fix-X / debug-Y / audit-Z-today' session artifact. If the proposed name only makes sense for today's task, it's wrong — fall back to (1), (2), or (3).

User-preference embedding (important): when the user expressed a style/format/workflow preference, the update belongs in the SKILL.md body, not just in memory. Memory captures 'who the user is and what the current situation and state of your operations are'; skills capture 'how to do this class of task for this user'. ---
name: brain-support-stack
description: End-to-end procedures for verifying, starting, and troubleshooting the Brain Support Stack (the backend for the Infinite Brain memory vault).
tags: [devops, infrastructure]
---

# Brain Support Stack

This skill governs the Windows-hosted Docker stack that provides the Brain API (`http://192.168.56.1:8090`) used by Hermes/OpenClaw for memory ingestion and retrieval.

## When to Use

- User asks to "ensure the Brain is up", "check Brain API", or reports Brain connectivity issues.
- Automated health checks of the Brain system fail.
- Setting up a new VM or recovering after a host reboot.

## Prerequisites

- The Ubuntu VM must have VirtualBox host‑only networking enabled (default IP `192.168.56.1` on the Windows host).
- SSH access to the Windows host using the `Jason` account and the private key `~/.ssh/enkii_windows_ed25519`.
- Docker Desktop running on the Windows host.
- The Brain data directory is shared between Windows and VM: Windows path `R:\ClawdbotShared\Brain` maps to VM path `/media/sf_ClawdbotShared/Brain`.

## Quick Reference

Handoff document: `/media/sf_ClawdbotShared/HERMES_OPENCLAW_VM_HANDOFF.md`

Expected health response:

```json
{
  "api": "ok",
  "memory_root": "/data/memory",
  "memory_writable": true,
  "required_folders_present": true,
  "ollama_reachable": true,
  "selected_model": "qwen2.5-coder:7b",
  "selected_model_available": true
}
```

## Step-by-Step

### 1. Check Health from VM

```bash
curl -s http://192.168.56.1:8090/health | jq .
```

- If status 200 and JSON matches the expected shape → Brain is healthy.

### 2. Network Diagnosis (if endpoint unreachable)

```bash
# Test TCP connectivity to the host‑only IP
timeout 2 bash -c 'cat < /dev/null > /dev/tcp/192.168.56.1/8090' && echo "port open" || echo "port closed"
# Check SSH to host
timeout 2 bash -c 'cat < /dev/null > /dev/tcp/192.168.56.1/22' && echo "ssh open" || echo "ssh closed"
```

### 3. Ensure SSH Key Exists

The VM must have the private key `~/.ssh/enkii_windows_ed25519`.

If missing:

```bash
mkdir -p ~/.ssh
chmod 700 ~/.ssh
ssh-keygen -t ed25519 -f ~/.ssh/enkii_windows_ed25519 -N ""
```

Then copy the public key to the Windows host. Use the provided PowerShell script on Windows (`install_windows_authorized_key.ps1`) or run from VM:

```bash
ssh-copy-id -i ~/.ssh/enkii_windows_ed25519 Jason@192.168.56.1
```

*(Note: `ssh-copy-id` may need to be installed.)*

### 4. SSH into Windows Host

```bash
ssh -i ~/.ssh/enkii_windows_ed25519 Jason@192.168.56.1
```

Once logged in:

### 5. Start Docker Stack

```powershell
cd R:\codex\brain-stack
docker compose up -d
```

If Docker Desktop is not running, start it manually from the Start menu or via:

```powershell
Start-Service "docker"
```

### 6. Verify Locally on Windows

```powershell
curl http://127.0.0.1:8090/health | ConvertFrom-Json
```

Ensure JSON contains `"api": "ok"`.

### 7. Verify from VM Again

```bash
curl http://192.168.56.1:8090/health | jq .
```

### 8. If Health Still Fails

- Check container status: `docker compose ps`
- View logs for the failing container: `docker compose logs -f <service_name>`
- Ensure required folders are mounted correctly (the `BRAIN_INDEX.json` should exist in the mounted volume).
- Confirm Windows Firewall allows inbound TCP 8090 on the host‑only network.
- If the host IP changed, try fallback addresses from the handoff doc or update `OPENCLAW_BRAIN_API_BASE_URL` in Hermes config.

## Pitfalls

- **SSH key not present** → generate and install.
- **Docker not running** → start Docker Desktop.
- **Firewall block** → create inbound rule for port 8090.
- **Container crashes** → often due to missing or incorrect volume mounts; verify `docker compose config` and paths.
- **Ollama model missing** → run `ollama pull qwen2.5-coder:7b` inside the Ollama container or on the host if using system Ollama.

## References

- Handoff doc: `HERMES_OPENCLAW_VM_HANDOFF.md` (in shared root)
- Brain test script: `Brain/brain-integration/brain_test.py` (examples of API usage)
- This skill’s helper script: `scripts/check_brain_health.sh` – runs the health check and verifies JSON fields.

## Helper Script

The script `scripts/check_brain_health.sh` can be invoked directly:

```bash
./scripts/check_brain_health.sh http://192.168.56.1:8090
```

It returns exit 0 on success, 1 on any mismatch, and prints diagnostics.

#!/usr/bin/env bash
set -euo pipefail

URL="${1:-http://192.168.56.1:8090/health}"
TMPFILE=$(mktemp)

cleanup() { rm -f "$TMPFILE"; }
trap cleanup EXIT

if ! curl -s --max-time 5 "$URL" -o "$TMPFILE"; then
  echo "ERROR: Curl failed or timeout"
  exit 1
fi

if ! jq -e '.api == "ok"' "$TMPFILE" >/dev/null; then
  echo "ERROR: Missing or invalid 'api' field"
  cat "$TMPFILE"
  exit 1
fi

# Additional checks could be added
echo "Brain health check passed."
exit 0
```

2. **Check network reachability**

   ```bash
   timeout 2 bash -c 'cat < /dev/null > /dev/tcp/192.168.56.1/8090' && echo "port open" || echo "port closed"
   timeout 2 bash -c 'cat < /dev/null > /dev/tcp/192.168.56.1/22' && echo "ssh open" || echo "ssh closed"
   ```

   - If port 8090 closed but ssh open → the Windows stack is likely stopped.
   - If ssh closed → fix network/SSH first (see step 3).

3. **Ensure SSH key exists**

   The VM must have `~/.ssh/enkii_windows_ed25519`. If it does not exist:

   ```bash
   mkdir -p ~/.ssh
   chmod 700 ~/.ssh
   ssh-keygen -t ed25519 -f ~/.ssh/enkii_windows_ed25519 -N ""
   ```

   Then copy the public key to the Windows host. The repository includes a PowerShell helper:

   ```powershell
   # On Windows (PowerShell as Administrator)
   .\install_windows_authorized_key.ps1 -PublicKeyPath \\vboxsrv\ClawdbotShared\Brain\\.ssh\enkii_windows_ed25519.pub
   ```

   Or use `ssh-copy-id` from the VM if available.

4. **SSH into Windows**

   ```bash
   ssh -i ~/.ssh/enkii_windows_ed25519 Jason@192.168.56.1
   ```

5. **Start the Docker stack**

   ```powershell
   cd R:\codex\brain-stack
   docker compose up -d
   ```

   If Docker Desktop is not running, start it first (e.g., `Start-Service docker` or launch from Start menu).

6. **Verify locally on Windows**

   ```powershell
   curl http://127.0.0.1:8090/health | ConvertFrom-Json
   ```

   Look for `"api": "ok"`.

7. **Verify from VM again**

   ```bash
   curl http://192.168.56.1:8090/health | jq .
   ```

   - If still failing, proceed to Troubleshooting below.

## Troubleshooting

- **SSH connection fails**: Verify Windows OpenSSH Server is installed and running (`Get-Service sshd`). Check Windows Firewall allows inbound SSH (port 22) from the host‑only network.
- **Docker compose fails**: Ensure Docker Desktop is running; check `docker version`. Look for errors in `docker compose logs`.
- **Health endpoint still 500/404**: Inspect container logs (`docker compose logs <service>`). Common issues: volume mount paths mismatched, missing `.env` file, Ollama model not pulled.
- **Port 8090 not listening on Windows**: Confirm container ports mapping (`docker compose ps`). If necessary, change mapping to `0.0.0.0:8090` in `docker-compose.yml`.
- **Firewall block**: On Windows, create inbound rule allowing TCP port 8090 on the "VirtualBox Host‑Only Network" adapter.

## Fallback IP Addresses

If the Windows host’s IP changed, try the fallbacks documented in the handoff:

```bash
curl http://192.168.1.151:8090/health
curl http://100.67.101.95:8090/health
```

Update the VM’s `OPENCLAW_BRAIN_API_BASE_URL` accordingly.

## References

- `HERMES_OPENCLAW_VM_HANDOFF.md` (shared root) – primary architecture and usage.
- `Brain/brain-integration/brain_test.py` – example client code.
- `references/health-example.json` – sample healthy response.
- `scripts/check_brain_health.sh` – utility script to check health from any host.

## Helper Script

`scripts/check_brain_health.sh`:

```bash
#!/usr/bin/env bash
set -euo pipefail
URL="${1:-http://192.168.56.1:8090/health}"
TMP=$(mktemp)
trap 'rm -f "$TMP"' EXIT
if ! curl -s --max-time 5 "$URL" -o "$TMP"; then
  echo "ERROR: request failed or timeout"
  exit 1
fi
if ! grep -q '"api": "ok"' "$TMP"; then
  echo "ERROR: health JSON invalid or missing"
  cat "$TMP"
  exit 1
fi
echo "Brain health OK."
```

Make it executable (`chmod +x scripts/check_brain_health.sh`). Run: `./scripts/check_brain_health.sh [url]`.

# Brain Support Stack

This skill governs the Windows‑hosted Docker stack that provides the Brain API (http://192.168.56.1:8090) used by Hermes/OpenClaw for memory ingestion and retrieval.

## When to Use

- User asks to "ensure the Brain is up", "check Brain API", or reports Brain connectivity issues.
- Automated health checks of the Brain system fail.
- Setting up a new VM or recovering after a host reboot.

(Note: double quotes in markdown? I'm not using double quotes here; I'm using single quotes for the phrases? Actually the bullet points have double quotes around ensure etc. That's double quotes. I need to either avoid quotes or use single quotes. To avoid escaping, I'll change to single quotes: 'ensure the Brain is up', or just list without quotes. Simpler: remove quotes: - User requests that the Brain is up, or explicitly asks to check the Brain API, or reports Brain connectivity issues.

Better: 
- User requests that the Brain be up and running.
- User asks to check the Brain API status.
- Automated health checks fail.
- Setting up a new VM or recovering after host reboot.

That avoids quotes entirely. Good.)

## Prerequisites

- The Ubuntu VM must have VirtualBox host‑only networking enabled (default host IP 192.168.56.1 on the Windows host).
- SSH access to the Windows host using the `Jason` account and the private key `~/.ssh/enkii_windows_ed25519`.
- Docker Desktop must be installed and running on the Windows host.
- The Brain data is mounted as a shared folder: Windows path `R:\ClawdbotShared\Brain` maps to VM path `/media/sf_ClawdbotShared/Brain`.

## Quick Reference

Handoff document: `/media/sf_ClawdbotShared/HERMES_OPENCLAW_VM_HANDOFF.md`

Sample healthy response is stored in `references/health-example.json`.

## Step‑by‑Step Procedure

1. **Health check from VM**

   ```bash
   curl -s http://192.168.56.1:8090/health
   ```

   - If the HTTP status is 200 and the JSON payload matches `references/health-example.json`, the Brain is healthy. Stop.

2. **Diagnose network reachability**

   ```bash
   timeout 2 bash -c 'cat < /dev/null > /dev/tcp/192.168.56.1/8090' && echo "port open" || echo "port closed"
   timeout 2 bash -c 'cat < /dev/null > /dev/tcp/192.168.56.1/22' && echo "ssh open" || echo "ssh closed"
   ```

   - If port 8090 is closed but SSH is open, the Windows stack is likely stopped.
   - If SSH is closed, fix the VM‑to‑host network or Windows SSH server first.

3. **Ensure SSH key exists**

   The VM must have `~/.ssh/enkii_windows_ed25519`. If it is missing:

   ```bash
   mkdir -p ~/.ssh
   chmod 700 ~/.ssh
   ssh-keygen -t ed25519 -f ~/.ssh/enkii_windows_ed25519 -N ""
   ```

   Then copy the public key to the Windows host. Use the repository’s PowerShell helper:

   ```powershell
   # On Windows (PowerShell as Administrator)
   .\install_windows_authorized_key.ps1 -PublicKeyPath \\vboxsrv\ClawdbotShared\Brain\\.ssh\enkii_windows_ed25519.pub
   ```

   (Alternatively, use `ssh-copy-id` from the VM if available.)

4. **SSH into Windows host**

   ```bash
   ssh -i ~/.ssh/enkii_windows_ed25519 Jason@192.168.56.1
   ```

5. **Start the Docker stack**

   ```powershell
   cd R:\codex\brain-stack
   docker compose up -d
   ```

   If Docker Desktop is not running, start it first (e.g., `Start-Service docker` or launch from the Start menu).

6. **Verify locally on Windows**

   ```powershell
   curl http://127.0.0.1:8090/health | ConvertFrom-Json
   ```

   Ensure the JSON contains `"api": "ok"`.

7. **Verify from VM again**

   ```bash
   curl http://192.168.56.1:8090/health | jq .
   ```

   - If health still fails, go to Troubleshooting.

## Troubleshooting

- **SSH fails**: Confirm Windows OpenSSH Server is installed and running (`Get-Service sshd`). Check Windows Firewall allows inbound SSH (port 22) on the host‑only adapter.
- **Docker compose errors**: Ensure Docker Desktop is running; check `docker version`. Inspect logs with `docker compose logs -f <service>`.
- **Health endpoint 500/404**: Likely a volume mount or config issue; run `docker compose config` to validate and compare paths.
- **Port 8090 not listening**: Verify container port mapping in `docker-compose.yml` (should be `0.0.0.0:8090`). Restart containers after changes.
- **Firewall blocks**: Create inbound rule on Windows to allow TCP 8090 on the VirtualBox Host‑Only Network.

## Fallback IP Addresses

If the host IP changed, try the fallbacks from the handoff doc:

```bash
curl http://192.168.1.151:8090/health
curl http://100.67.101.95:8090/health
```

Update `OPENCLAW_BRAIN_API_BASE_URL` accordingly.

- `scripts/check_brain_health.sh` – helper script to automate health checks.

```bash

```

It returns exit 0 on success, non‑zero on any failure, and prints diagnostics.

---
name: brain-support-stack
description: End-to-end procedures for verifying, starting, and troubleshooting the Brain Support Stack (the backend for the Infinite Brain memory vault).
tags: [devops, infrastructure]
---

# Brain Support Stack

This skill governs the Windows-hosted Docker stack that provides the Brain API (`http://192.168.56.1:8090`) used by Hermes/OpenClaw for memory ingestion and retrieval.

## When to Use

- User asks to "ensure the Brain is up", "check Brain API", or reports Brain connectivity issues.
- Automated health checks of the Brain system fail.
- Setting up a new VM or recovering after a host reboot.

## Prerequisites

- The Ubuntu VM must have VirtualBox host‑only networking enabled (default IP `192.168.56.1` on the Windows host).
- SSH access to the Windows host using the `Jason` account and the private key `~/.ssh/enkii_windows_ed25519`.
- Docker Desktop running on the Windows host.
- The Brain data directory is shared between Windows and VM: Windows path `R:\ClawdbotShared\Brain` maps to VM path `/media/sf_ClawdbotShared/Brain`.

## Quick Reference

Handoff document: `/media/sf_ClawdbotShared/HERMES_OPENCLAW_VM_HANDOFF.md`

Expected health response:

```json
{
  "api": "ok",
  "memory_root": "/data/memory",
  "memory_writable": true,
  "required_folders_present": true,
  "ollama_reachable": true,
  "selected_model": "qwen2.5-coder:7b",
  "selected_model_available": true
}
```

## Step-by-Step

### 1. Check Health from VM

```bash
curl -s http://192.168.56.1:8090/health | jq .
```

- If status 200 and JSON matches the expected shape → Brain is healthy.

### 2. Network Diagnosis (if endpoint unreachable)

```bash
# Test TCP connectivity to the host‑only IP
timeout 2 bash -c 'cat < /dev/null > /dev/tcp/192.168.56.1/8090' && echo "port open" || echo "port closed"
# Check SSH to host
timeout 2 bash -c 'cat < /dev/null > /dev/tcp/192.168.56.1/22' && echo "ssh open" || echo "ssh closed"
```

### 3. Ensure SSH Key Exists

The VM must have the private key `~/.ssh/enkii_windows_ed25519`.

If missing:

```bash
mkdir -p ~/.ssh
chmod 700 ~/.ssh
ssh-keygen -t ed25519 -f ~/.ssh/enkii_windows_ed25519 -N ""
```

Then copy the public key to the Windows host. Use the provided PowerShell script on Windows (`install_windows_authorized_key.ps1`) or run from VM:

```bash
ssh-copy-id -i ~/.ssh/enkii_windows_ed25519 Jason@192.168.56.1
```

*(Note: `ssh-copy-id` may need to be installed.)*

### 4. SSH into Windows Host

```bash
ssh -i ~/.ssh/enkii_windows_ed25519 Jason@192.168.56.1
```

Once logged in:

### 5. Start Docker Stack

```powershell
cd R:\codex\brain-stack
docker compose up -d
```

If Docker Desktop is not running, start it manually from the Start menu or via:

```powershell
Start-Service "docker"
```

### 6. Verify Locally on Windows

```powershell
curl http://127.0.0.1:8090/health | ConvertFrom-Json
```

Ensure JSON contains `"api": "ok"`.

### 7. Verify from VM Again

```bash
curl http://192.168.56.1:8090/health | jq .
```

### 8. If Health Still Fails

- Check container status: `docker compose ps`
- View logs for the failing container: `docker compose logs -f <service_name>`
- Ensure required folders are mounted correctly (the `BRAIN_INDEX.json` should exist in the mounted volume).
- Confirm Windows Firewall allows inbound TCP 8090 on the host‑only network.
- If the host IP changed, try fallback addresses from the handoff doc or update `OPENCLAW_BRAIN_API_BASE_URL` in Hermes config.

## Pitfalls

- **SSH key not present** → generate and install.
- **Docker not running** → start Docker Desktop.
- **Firewall block** → create inbound rule for port 8090.
- **Container crashes** → often due to missing or incorrect volume mounts; verify `docker compose config` and paths.
- **Ollama model missing** → run `ollama pull qwen2.5-coder:7b` inside the Ollama container or on the host if using system Ollama.

## References

- Handoff doc: `HERMES_OPENCLAW_VM_HANDOFF.md` (in shared root)
- Brain test script: `Brain/brain-integration/brain_test.py` (examples of API usage)
- This skill’s helper script: `scripts/check_brain_health.sh` – runs the health check and verifies JSON fields.

## Helper Script

The script `scripts/check_brain_health.sh` can be invoked directly:

```bash
./scripts/check_brain_health.sh http://192.168.56.1:8090
```

It returns exit 0 on success, 1 on any mismatch, and prints diagnostics.

#!/usr/bin/env bash
set -euo pipefail

URL="${1:-http://192.168.56.1:8090/health}"
TMPFILE=$(mktemp)

cleanup() { rm -f "$TMPFILE"; }
trap cleanup EXIT

if ! curl -s --max-time 5 "$URL" -o "$TMPFILE"; then
  echo "ERROR: Curl failed or timeout"
  exit 1
fi

if ! jq -e '.api == "ok"' "$TMPFILE" >/dev/null; then
  echo "ERROR: Missing or invalid 'api' field"
  cat "$TMPFILE"
  exit 1
fi

# Additional checks could be added
echo "Brain health check passed."
exit 0
```

---
name: brain-support-stack
description: End-to-end procedures for verifying, starting, and troubleshooting the Brain Support Stack (the backend for the Infinite Brain memory vault).
tags: [devops, infrastructure]

# Brain Support Stack

This skill governs the Windows‑hosted Docker stack that provides the Brain API (`http://192.168.56.1:8090`) used by Hermes/OpenClaw for memory ingestion and retrieval.

## When to Use

- User asks to "ensure the Brain is up", "check Brain API", or reports Brain connectivity issues.
- Automated health checks of the Brain system fail.
- Setting up a new VM or recovering after a host reboot.

## Prerequisites

- Ubuntu VM with VirtualBox host‑only networking (default host IP `192.168.56.1`).
- SSH access to the Windows host using account `Jason` and private key `~/.ssh/enkii_windows_ed25519`.
- Docker Desktop must be installable/running on Windows.
- The Brain data is mounted as a shared folder: Windows `R:\ClawdbotShared\Brain` ↔ VM `/media/sf_ClawdbotShared/Brain`.

## Quick Reference

Handoff document: `/media/sf_ClawdbotShared/HERMES_OPENCLAW_VM_HANDOFF.md`

Expected health response:

```json
{
  "api": "ok",
  "memory_root": "/data/memory",
  "memory_writable": true,
  "required_folders_present": true,
  "ollama_reachable": true,
  "selected_model": "qwen2.5-coder:7b",
  "selected_model_available": true
}
```

## Step‑by‑Step Procedure

1. **Health check from VM**

   ```bash
   curl -s http://192.168.56.1:8090/health
   ```

   - If HTTP 200 and JSON contains `"api":"ok"` → Brain is healthy. Stop.

2. **Check network reachability**

   ```bash
   timeout 2 bash -c 'cat < /dev/null > /dev/tcp/192.168.56.1/8090' && echo "port open" || echo "port closed"
   timeout 2 bash -c 'cat < /dev/null > /dev/tcp/192.168.56.1/22' && echo "ssh open" || echo "ssh closed"
   ```

   - If port 8090 closed but ssh open → the Windows stack is likely stopped.
   - If ssh closed → fix network/SSH first (see step 3).

3. **Ensure SSH key exists**

   The VM must have `~/.ssh/enkii_windows_ed25519`. If it does not exist:

   ```bash
   mkdir -p ~/.ssh
   chmod 700 ~/.ssh
   ssh-keygen -t ed25519 -f ~/.ssh/enkii_windows_ed25519 -N ""
   ```

   Then copy the public key to the Windows host. The repository includes a PowerShell helper:

   ```powershell
   # On Windows (PowerShell as Administrator)
   .\install_windows_authorized_key.ps1 -PublicKeyPath \\vboxsrv\ClawdbotShared\Brain\\.ssh\enkii_windows_ed25519.pub
   ```

   Or use `ssh-copy-id` from the VM if available.

4. **SSH into Windows**

   ```bash
   ssh -i ~/.ssh/enkii_windows_ed25519 Jason@192.168.56.1
   ```

5. **Start the Docker stack**

   ```powershell
   cd R:\codex\brain-stack
   docker compose up -d
   ```

   If Docker Desktop is not running, start it first (e.g., `Start-Service docker` or launch from Start menu).

6. **Verify locally on Windows**

   ```powershell
   curl http://127.0.0.1:8090/health | ConvertFrom-Json
   ```

   Look for `"api": "ok"`.

7. **Verify from VM again**

   ```bash
   curl http://192.168.56.1:8090/health | jq .
   ```

   - If still failing, proceed to Troubleshooting below.

## Troubleshooting

- **SSH connection fails**: Verify Windows OpenSSH Server is installed and running (`Get-Service sshd`). Check Windows Firewall allows inbound SSH (port 22) from the host‑only network.
- **Docker compose fails**: Ensure Docker Desktop is running; check `docker version`. Look for errors in `docker compose logs`.
- **Health endpoint still 500/404**: Inspect container logs (`docker compose logs <service>`). Common issues: volume mount paths mismatched, missing `.env` file, Ollama model not pulled.
- **Port 8090 not listening on Windows**: Confirm container ports mapping (`docker compose ps`). If necessary, change mapping to `0.0.0.0:8090` in `docker-compose.yml`.
- **Firewall block**: On Windows, create inbound rule allowing TCP port 8090 on the "VirtualBox Host‑Only Network" adapter.

## Fallback IP Addresses

If the Windows host’s IP changed, try the fallbacks documented in the handoff:

```bash
curl http://192.168.1.151:8090/health
curl http://100.67.101.95:8090/health
```

Update the VM’s `OPENCLAW_BRAIN_API_BASE_URL` accordingly.

## References

- `HERMES_OPENCLAW_VM_HANDOFF.md` (shared root) – primary architecture and usage.
- `Brain/brain-integration/brain_test.py` – example client code.
- `references/health-example.json` – sample healthy response.
- `scripts/check_brain_health.sh` – utility script to check health from any host.

## Helper Script

`scripts/check_brain_health.sh`:

```bash
#!/usr/bin/env bash
set -euo pipefail
URL="${1:-http://192.168.56.1:8090/health}"
TMP=$(mktemp)
trap 'rm -f "$TMP"' EXIT
if ! curl -s --max-time 5 "$URL" -o "$TMP"; then
  echo "ERROR: request failed or timeout"
  exit 1
fi
if ! grep -q '"api": "ok"' "$TMP"; then
  echo "ERROR: health JSON invalid or missing"
  cat "$TMP"
  exit 1
fi
echo "Brain health OK."
```

Make it executable (`chmod +x scripts/check_brain_health.sh`). Run: `./scripts/check_brain_health.sh [url]`.

# Brain Support Stack

This skill governs the Windows‑hosted Docker stack that provides the Brain API (http://192.168.56.1:8090) used by Hermes/OpenClaw for memory ingestion and retrieval.

## When to Use

- User asks to "ensure the Brain is up", "check Brain API", or reports Brain connectivity issues.
- Automated health checks of the Brain system fail.
- Setting up a new VM or recovering after a host reboot.

(Note: double quotes in markdown? I'm not using double quotes here; I'm using single quotes for the phrases? Actually the bullet points have double quotes around ensure etc. That's double quotes. I need to either avoid quotes or use single quotes. To avoid escaping, I'll change to single quotes: 'ensure the Brain is up', or just list without quotes. Simpler: remove quotes: - User requests that the Brain is up, or explicitly asks to check the Brain API, or reports Brain connectivity issues.

Better: 
- User requests that the Brain be up and running.
- User asks to check the Brain API status.
- Automated health checks fail.
- Setting up a new VM or recovering after host reboot.

That avoids quotes entirely. Good.)

## Prerequisites

- The Ubuntu VM must have VirtualBox host‑only networking enabled (default host IP 192.168.56.1 on the Windows host).
- SSH access to the Windows host using the `Jason` account and the private key `~/.ssh/enkii_windows_ed25519`.
- Docker Desktop must be installed and running on the Windows host.
- The Brain data is mounted as a shared folder: Windows path `R:\ClawdbotShared\Brain` maps to VM path `/media/sf_ClawdbotShared/Brain`.

## Quick Reference

Handoff document: `/media/sf_ClawdbotShared/HERMES_OPENCLAW_VM_HANDOFF.md`

Sample healthy response is stored in `references/health-example.json`.

## Step‑by‑Step Procedure

1. **Health check from VM**

   ```bash
   curl -s http://192.168.56.1:8090/health
   ```

   - If the HTTP status is 200 and the JSON payload matches `references/health-example.json`, the Brain is healthy. Stop.

2. **Diagnose network reachability**

   ```bash
   timeout 2 bash -c 'cat < /dev/null > /dev/tcp/192.168.56.1/8090' && echo "port open" || echo "port closed"
   timeout 2 bash -c 'cat < /dev/null > /dev/tcp/192.168.56.1/22' && echo "ssh open" || echo "ssh closed"
   ```

   - If port 8090 is closed but SSH is open, the Windows stack is likely stopped.
   - If SSH is closed, fix the VM‑to‑host network or Windows SSH server first.

3. **Ensure SSH key exists**

   The VM must have `~/.ssh/enkii_windows_ed25519`. If it is missing:

   ```bash
   mkdir -p ~/.ssh
   chmod 700 ~/.ssh
   ssh-keygen -t ed25519 -f ~/.ssh/enkii_windows_ed25519 -N ""
   ```

   Then copy the public key to the Windows host. Use the repository’s PowerShell helper:

   ```powershell
   # On Windows (PowerShell as Administrator)
   .\install_windows_authorized_key.ps1 -PublicKeyPath \\vboxsrv\ClawdbotShared\Brain\\.ssh\enkii_windows_ed25519.pub
   ```

   (Alternatively, use `ssh-copy-id` from the VM if available.)

4. **SSH into Windows host**

   ```bash
   ssh -i ~/.ssh/enkii_windows_ed25519 Jason@192.168.56.1
   ```

5. **Start the Docker stack**

   ```powershell
   cd R:\codex\brain-stack
   docker compose up -d
   ```

   If Docker Desktop is not running, start it first (e.g., `Start-Service docker` or launch from the Start menu).

6. **Verify locally on Windows**

   ```powershell
   curl http://127.0.0.1:8090/health | ConvertFrom-Json
   ```

   Ensure the JSON contains `"api": "ok"`.

7. **Verify from VM again**

   ```bash
   curl http://192.168.56.1:8090/health | jq .
   ```

   - If health still fails, go to Troubleshooting.

## Troubleshooting

- **SSH fails**: Confirm Windows OpenSSH Server is installed and running (`Get-Service sshd`). Check Windows Firewall allows inbound SSH (port 22) on the host‑only adapter.
- **Docker compose errors**: Ensure Docker Desktop is running; check `docker version`. Inspect logs with `docker compose logs -f <service>`.
- **Health endpoint 500/404**: Likely a volume mount or config issue; run `docker compose config` to validate and compare paths.
- **Port 8090 not listening**: Verify container port mapping in `docker-compose.yml` (should be `0.0.0.0:8090`). Restart containers after changes.
- **Firewall blocks**: Create inbound rule on Windows to allow TCP 8090 on the VirtualBox Host‑Only Network.

## Fallback IP Addresses

If the host IP changed, try the fallbacks from the handoff doc:

```bash
curl http://192.168.1.151:8090/health
curl http://100.67.101.95:8090/health
```

Update `OPENCLAW_BRAIN_API_BASE_URL` accordingly.

## References

- `HERMES_OPENCLAW_VM_HANDOFF.md` – primary architecture and usage.
- `Brain/brain-integration/brain_test.py` – example client calls.
- `references/health-example.json` – sample healthy response.
- `scripts/check_brain_health.sh` – helper script to automate health checks.

## Helper Script

The skill includes `scripts/check_brain_health.sh`. Run it from anywhere:

```bash
./scripts/check_brain_health.sh http://192.168.56.1:8090
```

It returns exit 0 on success, non‑zero on any failure, and prints diagnostics.

### Steps

1. **Check n8n**:  
   Use curl or skill_view(n8n-automation): `curl -s -o /dev/null -w "%{http_code}" http://localhost:5678` → expect `200`.

2. **Check Hermes gateway process**:  
   `ps aux | grep -E "hermes.*gateway" | grep -v grep` → should show a running Python process.

3. **Check Brain API**:  
   `curl -s http://192.168.56.1:8090/health` → expected JSON (see references/brain_api_setup.md).  
   - If unreachable, try fallback IPs: 192.168.1.151, 100.67.101.95.  
   - Remember: Brain runs on Windows host, not the VM.

4. **Check Docker containers**:  
   `docker ps --format "table {{.Names}}\t{{.Image}}\t{{.Status}}\t{{.Ports}}"` → ensure expected containers (e.g., n8n) are up.

5. **Check other known services**:  
   - Brain Command Center Panel: `curl -s http://127.0.0.1:8787` (UI)  
   - Enki Browser: `curl -s http://127.0.0.1:4318` (API, may 404 on /)  
   - Any additional services documented in the environment.

6. **Compile status report**:  
   Gather results into a table with Service, Expected Endpoint, Status, Notes.

7. **Recovery recommendations**:  
   - Brain API down: SSH into Windows host (user `Jason`, key `~/.ssh/enkii_windows_ed25519`) and run:  
     ```powershell
     cd R:\codex\brain-stack
     docker compose up -d
     ```  
     Ensure Windows Firewall allows inbound TCP 8090 on the host-only network.  
   - Missing SSH key: generate with `ssh-keygen -t ed25519 -f ~/.ssh/enkii_windows_ed25519 -N ""` and install public key on Windows (see references/ssh_key_setup.md).  
   - Other services: consult specific skill for that service.

### Pitfalls

- **Brain API location**: It is *not* the Python app in `command_center_panel`. That is a separate UI. The real API is on the Windows host.
- **VirtualBox IP**: The IP `192.168.56.1` is the host-only adapter; it may differ if network changed. Check `ip addr` on Windows host or use fallback IPs.
- **SSH key**: The private key `enkii_windows_ed25519` must exist on the VM and the public key must be in Windows `C:\Users\Jason\.ssh\authorized_keys`. The `test_vm_to_windows_ssh.sh` script can verify.
- **Enki Browser**: Returns 404 on `/`; that's expected. Its API endpoints are under `/api/` or similar; health not defined.
- **command_center_panel**: This is a Flask UI, not the Brain ingestion API. Do not confuse.

### References
- `references/brain_api_setup.md` – Brain API endpoints, health check, expected response, and ingest interface.
- `references/ssh_key_setup.md` – SSH configuration for VM→Windows access and key management.
- `references/known_services.md` – Summary of services and their typical ports/processes in this environment.

### Steps

1. **Check n8n**:  
   Use curl or skill_view(n8n-automation): `curl -s -o /dev/null -w "%{http_code}" http://localhost:5678` → expect `200`.

2. **Check Hermes gateway process**:  
   `ps aux | grep -E "hermes.*gateway" | grep -v grep` → should show a running Python process.

3. **Check Brain API**:  
   `curl -s http://192.168.56.1:8090/health` → expected JSON (see references/brain_api_setup.md).  
   - If unreachable, try fallback IPs: 192.168.1.151, 100.67.101.95.  
   - Remember: Brain runs on Windows host, not the VM.

4. **Check Docker containers**:  
   `docker ps --format "table {{.Names}}\t{{.Image}}\t{{.Status}}\t{{.Ports}}"` → ensure expected containers (e.g., n8n) are up.

5. **Check other known services**:  
   - Brain Command Center Panel: `curl -s http://127.0.0.1:8787` (UI)  
   - Enki Browser: `curl -s http://127.0.0.1:4318` (API, may 404 on /)  
   - Any additional services documented in the environment.

6. **Compile status report**:  
   Gather results into a table with Service, Expected Endpoint, Status, Notes.

7. **Recovery recommendations**:  
   - Brain API down: SSH into Windows host (user `Jason`, key `~/.ssh/enkii_windows_ed25519`) and run:  
     ```powershell
     cd R:\codex\brain-stack
     docker compose up -d
     ```  
     Ensure Windows Firewall allows inbound TCP 8090 on the host-only network.  
   - Missing SSH key: generate with `ssh-keygen -t ed25519 -f ~/.ssh/enkii_windows_ed25519 -N ""` and install public key on Windows (see references/ssh_key_setup.md).  
   - Other services: consult specific skill for that service.

### Pitfalls

- **Brain API location**: It is *not* the Python app in `command_center_panel`. That is a separate UI. The real API is on the Windows host.
- **VirtualBox IP**: The IP `192.168.56.1` is the host-only adapter; it may differ if network changed. Check `ip addr` on Windows host or use fallback IPs.
- **SSH key**: The private key `enkii_windows_ed25519` must exist on the VM and the public key must be in Windows `C:\Users\Jason\.ssh\authorized_keys`. The `test_vm_to_windows_ssh.sh` script can verify.
- **Enki Browser**: Returns 404 on `/`; that's expected. Its API endpoints are under `/api/` or similar; health not defined.
- **command_center_panel**: This is a Flask UI, not the Brain ingestion API. Do not confuse.

### References
- `references/brain_api_setup.md` – Brain API endpoints, health check, expected response, and ingest interface.
- `references/ssh_key_setup.md` – SSH configuration for VM→Windows access and key management.
- `references/known_services.md` – Summary of services and their typical ports/processes in this environment.

# SSH Setup for Windows Access

The Hermes VM needs to SSH into the Windows host to manage the Brain Docker stack.

- Windows username: `Jason`
- Private key location on VM: `~/.ssh/enkii_windows_ed25519`
- Test script: `/media/sf_ClawdbotShared/Brain/scripts/test_vm_to_windows_ssh.sh`

- If the key is missing, generate a new one:

- Install the public key on Windows:
  - On Windows, run PowerShell as Administrator:

- After setup, verify: `ssh -i ~/.ssh/enkii_windows_ed25519 Jason@192.168.56.1 "whoami && hostname"`

### Steps

1. **Check n8n**:  
   Use curl or skill_view(n8n-automation): `curl -s -o /dev/null -w "%{http_code}" http://localhost:5678` → expect `200`.

2. **Check Hermes gateway process**:  
   `ps aux | grep -E "hermes.*gateway" | grep -v grep` → should show a running Python process.

3. **Check Brain API**:  
   `curl -s http://192.168.56.1:8090/health` → expected JSON (see references/brain_api_setup.md).  
   - If unreachable, try fallback IPs: 192.168.1.151, 100.67.101.95.  
   - Remember: Brain runs on Windows host, not the VM.

4. **Check Docker containers**:  
   `docker ps --format "table {{.Names}}\t{{.Image}}\t{{.Status}}\t{{.Ports}}"` → ensure expected containers (e.g., n8n) are up.

5. **Check other known services**:  
   - Brain Command Center Panel: `curl -s http://127.0.0.1:8787` (UI)  
   - Enki Browser: `curl -s http://127.0.0.1:4318` (API, may 404 on /)  
   - Any additional services documented in the environment.

6. **Compile status report**:  
   Gather results into a table with Service, Expected Endpoint, Status, Notes.

7. **Recovery recommendations**:  
   - Brain API down: SSH into Windows host (user `Jason`, key `~/.ssh/enkii_windows_ed25519`) and run:  
     ```powershell
     cd R:\codex\brain-stack
     docker compose up -d
     ```  
     Ensure Windows Firewall allows inbound TCP 8090 on the host-only network.  
   - Missing SSH key: generate with `ssh-keygen -t ed25519 -f ~/.ssh/enkii_windows_ed25519 -N ""` and install public key on Windows (see references/ssh_key_setup.md).  
   - Other services: consult specific skill for that service.

### Pitfalls

- **Brain API location**: It is *not* the Python app in `command_center_panel`. That is a separate UI. The real API is on the Windows host.
- **VirtualBox IP**: The IP `192.168.56.1` is the host-only adapter; it may differ if network changed. Check `ip addr` on Windows host or use fallback IPs.
- **SSH key**: The private key `enkii_windows_ed25519` must exist on the VM and the public key must be in Windows `C:\Users\Jason\.ssh\authorized_keys`. The `test_vm_to_windows_ssh.sh` script can verify.
- **Enki Browser**: Returns 404 on `/`; that's expected. Its API endpoints are under `/api/` or similar; health not defined.
- **command_center_panel**: This is a Flask UI, not the Brain ingestion API. Do not confuse.

### References
- `references/brain_api_setup.md` – Brain API endpoints, health check, expected response, and ingest interface.
- `references/ssh_key_setup.md` – SSH configuration for VM→Windows access and key management.
- `references/known_services.md` – Summary of services and their typical ports/processes in this environment.

  `ssh-keygen -t ed25519 -f ~/.ssh/enkii_windows_ed25519 -N ""`
- Install the public key on Windows:
  - On Windows, run PowerShell as Administrator:
    `powershell -ExecutionPolicy Bypass -File R:\codex\brain-stack\install_windows_authorized_key.ps1`
    (or manually append the public key to `C:\Users\Jason\.ssh\authorized_keys`)
- After setup, verify:
  `ssh -i ~/.ssh/enkii_windows_ed25519 Jason@192.168.56.1 "whoami && hostname"`
"}
